---
title: "Lectures"
---

## Lecture notes

| Lecture | Topic | Files |
|---:|---|---|
| 1 | Introduction to process modeling | Coming soon |
| 2 | Thermodynamics I: Properties and Pure Fluid | Coming soon |
| 3 | Thermodynamics II: Mixtures | Coming soon |
| 4 | Energy Balances of multicomponent systems | Coming soon |
| 5 | Mixer and splitter models | Coming soon |
| 6 | Thermodynamics III: Vapor-liquid equilibrium | Coming soon |
| 7 | Flash calculations I: Simplified flash calculations | Coming soon |
| 8 | Flash calculations II: Rigorous flash calculations | Coming soon |
| 9 | Distillation: Rigorous distillation calculations | Coming soon |
| 10 | Fixed-conversion and kinetic-based reactor models | Coming soon |
| 11 | Thermodynamics IV: Chemical reaction equilibrium | Coming soon |
| 12 | Equilibrium-based reactors | Coming soon |
| 13 | Sequential modular approach for process simulation | Coming soon |
| 14 | Partition and tearing methods for process simulation | Coming soon |
| 15 | Equation-oriented approach for process simulation | Coming soon |
| 16 | Process optimization | Coming soon |
| 17 | Hybrid modeling and machine learning for process simulation | Coming soon |

## Example equation

A simple dynamic mass balance can be written as:

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(C_{in} - C) + r(C)
$$

where:

- $C$ is the concentration,
- $F$ is the flow rate,
- $V$ is the volume,
- $C_{in}$ is the inlet concentration,
- $r(C)$ is the reaction rate.
